one_line_takeaway: A security-aware systematization of federated unlearning that maps methods by who-unlearns / influence-removal / performance-recovery and flags the field's core weaknesses -- FedAvg+MNIST/CIFAR-10 monoculture, arbitrary non-IID simulation, no shared benchmark, and the under-examined risk that unlearning itself can leak the data it is meant to erase.
key_finding: The FU field is immature and methodologically fragmented. Concrete findings from the survey's own coding: (1) heavy over-reliance on simplistic setups -- ~53 of 94 studies evaluate primarily on MNIST/CIFAR-10, image datasets account for ~80% of all dataset usage (209 uses), and over 90% of works still use plain FedAvg aggregation despite its known intolerance to Byzantine failures and heterogeneity; (2) non-IID realism is weak -- most works simulate heterogeneity via a Dirichlet concentration parameter alpha whose values range arbitrarily from 0.1 to 5 with no consensus, preventing fair cross-paper comparison; (3) no common benchmark or unified 'unlearnability' metric exists, so evaluations are indirect (comparing unlearned vs. retrained models); (4) security is usually only THEORETICALLY claimed, not empirically validated, and backdoor-based efficacy evaluation is unreliable because simple backdoors naturally decay over training rounds -- only one work [75, Alam et al.] used stronger backdoors (Constraint-and-Scale, Neurotoxin); (5) FU is mostly confined to horizontal FL and vision tasks, with VFL, decentralized FL, and LLM unlearning largely unexplored.
core_contribution: This is a SURVEY (Jeong, Ma, Houmansadr; arXiv:2403.02437v3, 2025), not a new method. Its own contribution is a systematic, security-aware taxonomy and comparative analysis of the Federated Unlearning (FU) literature published since 2020 (~94 papers by March 2025). The authors' specific contributions are: (1) articulating why centralized machine-unlearning methods do NOT transfer trivially to FL, isolating four federated-specific complexities (interactive training, information isolation, non-IID data, stochastic client selection); (2) a 'who unlearns' analysis (server vs. target client vs. remaining clients) that ties each unlearner to the knowledge it can access (their Table II); (3) a two-axis taxonomy of unlearning mechanisms -- influence removal (historical information, data manipulation, gradient manipulation [perturbation/scaling/pruning], loss/influence approximation, knowledge distillation, multi-task learning, reverse training/gradient ascent, clustering, others) and performance recovery (post-training, fine-tuning, gradient manipulation, regularization, KD) -- populated across their Tables III-V; (4) a comparison of evaluation metrics across efficacy/fidelity/efficiency (Table VI); and (5) identification of open gaps. CRITICAL for grounding: named systems such as FedEraser, FedRecover, SIFU, FedRecovery, Ferrari, KNOT, TrustChain, Halimi et al., etc. are SURVEYED prior works, not this paper's own methods.
threat_model: The paper treats security/privacy as first-class but as a SURVEY-LEVEL SYNTHESIS across the field, plus one original threat observation of its own. Two distinct security angles are surveyed: (a) unlearning AS a defense -- removing the influence of backdoor-poisoned or compromised client data from a trained FL model (motivated by GDPR/CPRA right-to-be-forgotten and by model owners wanting to erase faulty/backdoored data); efficacy here is often measured via Attack Success Rate of backdoor attacks (BAs) and membership inference attacks (MIAs). (b) Unlearning AS an attack surface -- the authors' own emphasized point (Section VIII) is that FU can paradoxically DEGRADE privacy: because both the learned and unlearned models are shared among participants, an honest-but-curious server or remaining clients can diff the two to infer the very data that was supposed to be forgotten (via inference / model-inversion attacks). They give an explicit adversary scenario: honest-but-curious remaining clients/server where only the target client can verify successful unlearning; a malicious server can manipulate aggregated updates and extract sensitive data from target and remaining clients. They note only two surveyed works [35 RobustFU, 56 BadUnlearn] tackle poisoning-robust FU, and that no existing FU method has been rigorously analyzed for the diff-the-models leakage risk.
limitations: As a survey it introduces no new technique, benchmark, or empirical evidence -- it diagnoses gaps rather than closing them. Coverage is self-described as non-exhaustive (Google Scholar keyword search, cutoff ~March 2025). The comparison tables are categorical codings by the authors and inherit whatever the original papers reported; because the surveyed works use inconsistent datasets, architectures, alpha values, and metrics, the survey itself cannot produce head-to-head quantitative rankings of methods -- exactly the 'absence of a common benchmark' gap it identifies. Its own novel privacy-leakage threat (diffing learned vs. unlearned models) is presented as an unquantified conceptual scenario, not demonstrated with an attack.

datasets_used: MNIST and CIFAR-10 (dominant; ~53 of 94 surveyed studies used these simple image sets)
CIFAR-100, EMNIST, CelebA (less-common, more complex image sets)
FEMNIST, Tiny-Shakespeare, MovieLens-100k (naturally non-IID real-world sets used by surveyed works [28,30,78])
Multi-modal TCGA / TMI (used only twice, in a single surveyed work [28])
NOTE: these are datasets used ACROSS the surveyed FU literature, not by this survey itself, which runs no experiments
quantitative_results: ~94 FU papers by March 2025 (85 by end of 2024); field originated in 2020 with the first work [102]
~53 of 94 studies evaluated primarily on MNIST/CIFAR-10
Image datasets = ~80% of all dataset usage (209 total uses); multi-modal used only twice in one work
Over 90% of surveyed works use plain FedAvg aggregation
Dirichlet non-IID concentration parameter alpha ranges arbitrarily from 0.1 to 5 across papers (Table IX)
Only 2 works [35 RobustFU, 56 BadUnlearn] propose poisoning-robust FU
Only 1 work [75 Alam et al.] evaluated with advanced backdoors (Constraint-and-Scale, Neurotoxin)
Only a few works [80 FedHarmony, 94 FedLU, 30 KNOT] use non-FedAvg aggregation (FedProx/FedBuff/FedEqual); [2 FedRecover, 58 VeriFi] use Byzantine-robust rules (Krum, Trimmed-Mean)
baselines_compared: This paper is a survey; its 'baseline' comparison is against 8 prior unlearning/FU surveys in Table I: [3] Nguyen et al. 2022, [6] Xu et al. 2023, [7] Wu et al. 2023, [8] Wang et al. 2023, [9] Yang & Zhao 2023, [10] Liu et al. 2023, [11] Shaik et al. 2024, [12] Romandini et al. 2024

Concurrent survey [10] (Liu et al., ACM Computing Surveys) is identified as the closest prior work; this survey differentiates itself by depth on practicality, non-IID simulation, dataset-usage statistics, and security/privacy implications
No experimental baselines exist because the paper proposes no new method
